statement: When an outcome is a non-negative integer count (births per year, animals sighted, events in an interval), a standard model is Poisson regression: Y ~ Poisson(lambda), with log(lambda) = linear or polynomial in the covariates. For a single time covariate t, log(lambda) = alpha + beta1*t + beta2*t^2 + beta3*t^3 accommodates rising-then-falling or multi-phase trajectories. The Poisson mean equals its variance, so larger counts are automatically noisier in absolute terms.
source: standard GLM for count data; used widely in ecological monitoring

statement: Wildlife population time-series often show a characteristic boom-and-bust shape: initial rise, peak near a specific time, then decline to near-zero. Causes include habitat saturation plus a late stressor (disease, pesticide, habitat loss). For peregrine falcons specifically, organochlorine pesticides (DDT) caused a sharp mid-20th-century crash after an earlier rising baseline. Time-series can span 3-6 orders of magnitude from peak to trough, so log-scale modelling of rate parameters is appropriate.

formal.shape: unimodal with rising then falling rate; tail can be near-zero
formal.peak_timing_hint: typically first third of the observation window

source: wildlife-conservation time-series literature; Marchant et al. (2010) peregrine trends

statement: When the response's trajectory has at least one turning point (rise then fall, or vice versa), a low-order polynomial (quadratic or cubic) in the independent variable is often the simplest adequate fit. Cubic polynomials admit up to two turning points — useful when the data shows both an initial rise AND a subsequent dip or tail. Overfitting is controlled by putting priors on the coefficients (smaller coefficients = smoother trajectory).
